title: Day 03 — Reusable Form Components
summary: Master map, filter, and reduce for UI data, compose components with children, render lists with stable keys, and extract Day 02's login inputs into a reusable library.

blocks.content: Yesterday you built a login page with controlled inputs. Today you stop copying markup. Product companies — Stripe dashboards, Notion settings, Razorpay merchant panels — share input primitives across dozens of forms. You will learn the array methods that power dynamic UI, the children prop for flexible composition, and keys — the interview topic juniors miss until production bugs appear.

blocks.title: Scope for Day 03
blocks.content: Use only what you know from Days 01–02: components, props, useState, controlled inputs. Do not add useEffect, fetch, React Router, or custom hooks yet. Those are scheduled for Days 04–06.
Static JSX does not scale. Forms have dynamic error lists. Settings pages render ten toggles from config. Admin tables map rows to cells. Three array methods cover 90% of list UI prep in React apps.

map creates a new array by transforming every element. In React, the transformation is often JSX. You already saw map on Day 01 for feature cards — today you apply it to form field configs and error messages.

blocks.title: map vs forEach
blocks.content: forEach returns undefined — use it for side effects only. map returns a new array — use it when the result becomes JSX or derived data.
filter keeps elements that pass a test. After validation you might have optional error strings — filter removes empty ones before rendering a summary list.
reduce folds an array into a single value. Use it for counts — how many fields failed validation — or building a lookup map. You will use reduce more in Week 2 with carts and stats.

blocks.text: TypeScript — Reusable interfaces and generic props

blocks.content: Reusable components need shared prop shapes. Define a base interface once; extend it per component. Generics let one wrapper work with different value types later — optional today but interviewers love the vocabulary.

blocks.title: Extend, do not duplicate
blocks.content: If TextInput and PasswordInput share label + error + id, put those on BaseFieldProps. Divergent props stay on the specific type. This mirrors how design systems document APIs.

When you map an array to components, React needs a key on each sibling to track identity across re-renders. Missing keys cause console warnings. Wrong keys cause subtle bugs — cursor jumping, wrong error under wrong field, state leaking between rows.

rows: High — new key every render remounts everything

blocks.title: Key placement
blocks.content: key goes on the outermost element returned from map — not on a nested span inside. React only reads keys on direct children of the array.

children is the content between a component's opening and closing tags. Button uses children for label text. Card uses children for arbitrary body content. You avoid creating titleProp, bodyProp, footerProp for every layout variant.

Your TextInput should not hardcode email state. It receives value and onChange from the parent — the same controlled pattern from Day 02. The library stays dumb; LoginForm stays smart. This separation is how teams share UI across products.

blocks.code:
<TextInput
  id="email"
  label="Email"
  value={form.email}
  onChange={(email) => setForm((p) => ({ ...p, email }))}
  error={errors.email}
/>

blocks.content: Create TextInput, PasswordInput, Checkbox, Button, and Error components. Refactor your Day 02 login page to use them. Behavior must not regress — this is a refactor, not a rewrite.

items.text: TextInput with label, value, onChange, error
Checkbox with label and controlled checked state
Button with children, loading, and disabled states
Error component for field and summary messages
Login page uses library — no duplicated input markup
At least one list rendered with map() and stable keys

blocks.title: Hints, not solutions
blocks.content: Extract TextInput first and swap one field on the login form. Then PasswordInput, reusing label/error layout. Button children hold the sign-in label. Keep validation logic in LoginForm — only move presentation.

blocks.text: Debugging — mistakes you will make today

blocks.content: Symptom: console warns about unique keys. Cause: map without key or key on inner element. Fix: key={item.id} on the top-level element in the map return.
Symptom: wrong error under wrong field. Cause: misaligned arrays or unstable index keys after reorder. Fix: key by field id; derive visible errors with filter before map.
Symptom: styling fix requires many edits. Cause: copy-paste instead of shared TextInput. Fix: one source of truth in ui/ folder; forms pass props only.

items.text: Input library extracted and login refactored
